preFix.title: Installing mains drip irrigation with a hose timer
preFix.subtitle: Setting up a drip emitter system on a timer to automate vegetable bed watering
preFix.excerpt: Drip irrigation delivers water directly to the root zone at a rate of 1 to 4 litres per hour per emitter, compared to 10 to 15 litres per minute from a hand-held hose. Combined with a mechanical or digital timer, it reduces garden water use by 30 to 50 percent versus hand watering while producing comparable or better crop yields by maintaining consistent soil moisture.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS drip irrigation guidance 2024. Irrigation Association Water Management standards for residential systems. WRAP Water UK garden water saving guidance.

content.text: Drip emitters

deliver water at low pressure straight to the root zone. This cuts waste compared to a hose, which sprays water over paths, weeds, and foliage where it evaporates with no benefit. Emitters running at 2 litres per hour give the same weekly moisture to a plant as 14 litres of hand-watering, with no soil wasted.
A hose and drip system connected to the mains must include an approved backflow preventer between the tap and the timer. Most quality
hose timers

include a built-in check valve. If your timer does not, fit a WRAS-approved double-check valve between the tap and the timer. This prevents contaminated water siphoning back into the mains when the tap is turned off.
What you need
Hose timer (digital or mechanical) with built-in backflow preventer.
13 mm lay-flat or round main supply hose to run from the timer to the beds. Maximum practical run: 30 m at mains pressure.
4 mm micro-tube and
pressure-compensating emitters
at 2 litres per hour for individual plants. Or 13 mm
soaker hose
for row crops.
Hole punch tool for making 4 mm holes in the main supply hose to insert micro-tube connectors.

Drip emitters require a minimum of 0.5 bar to operate correctly. If the mains dynamic pressure is below 1.5 bar, use pressure-compensating emitters specifically rated for low-pressure operation. Soaker hoses perform adequately from 0.5 bar upward. For mains above 3.5 bar, fit a
flow restrictor
at the tap to avoid over-pressurising the emitter connections.
Installation
Screw the hose timer to the outdoor tap. Connect the 13 mm main supply hose to the timer outlet. Route the main hose along the bed edges, held with wire staples or hose clips.

Use the hole punch to make 4 mm holes in the main hose at each plant position. Insert a barbed connector in each hole and push a 300 mm length of 4 mm micro-tube onto the connector. Push a drip emitter onto the end of each micro-tube and position it 50 to 100 mm from the plant stem.
Close the end of the main hose with an end cap or figure-8 clip. Programme the timer to run for 30 to 60 minutes per session, once or twice per day depending on weather and crop type.

Run the system manually and check every emitter is flowing. Push a stick 100 mm into the soil near a representative plant immediately after a session. The soil should be wet to 100 mm depth. If it is not, either run longer or add a second emitter per plant.
What done well looks like

All emitters deliver water consistently. Soil moisture 100 mm below each emitter is consistently moist through dry spells. The timer has been set to run in the early morning at 06:00, avoiding afternoon evaporation. Crop growth in the drip-irrigated beds is equal to or better than hand-watered beds, and the water meter confirms a 40 percent reduction in garden water use during a dry July.
What done badly looks like

Emitters were placed 300 mm from the stem rather than 50 to 100 mm. In dry conditions the wetter zone around the emitter does not reach the root zone of the plant. Three emitters at the end of a 40 m non-compensated run deliver half the flow of the emitters at the start, causing drought stress in the far beds. The timer was not set back from the default programme during a wet week, overwatering and causing powdery mildew on courgettes.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Remove the blocked emitters and soak in white vinegar for 30 minutes. Rinse and refit. If the supply water is high in calcium, fit a sediment filter at the tap before the timer to extend emitter life.
attrs.cause: Emitters are blocked with mineral deposits or debris from the supply water
attrs.symptom: Some emitters are not flowing at all

attrs.fix: Use AA alkaline batteries rated at 2,850 mAh or above. Most timers consume 50 to 150 mAh per solenoid actuation; a quality battery should last a full growing season with two daily cycles.
attrs.cause: High-quality alkaline batteries are needed; carbon-zinc batteries have insufficient capacity for repeated solenoid actuation
attrs.symptom: Timer battery fails after only two weeks

attrs.fix: Reduce mains pressure at the tap with a flow restrictor to below 3 bar before the system. Alternatively, use locking barbed connectors rather than plain push-fit barbs, which hold securely at up to 4 bar.
attrs.cause: Mains pressure is too high for the 4 mm connector barbs
attrs.symptom: Main hose connections to micro-tubes have popped off
